coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages and their visible light photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Baoying
Yu, Xiang
Yu, Xuelian
Du, Ruifeng
Liu, Lei
Zhang, Yihe
coredata.dc:description: A novel graphene quantum dots (GQDs) decorated ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages ternary photocatalyst was prepared by a simple one-step deposition process. The as-prepared ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 were characterized by X-ray powder diffraction, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis, nitrogen absorption and desorption isothermal curves and electrochemical test. The synthesized GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance by degrading organic dye of methylene blue (MB) and tetracycline hydrochloride compared with ZnO and ZnO-ZnFe2O4. This study successfully shed a new thought on solving environmental problems by using low-cost, non-toxic metal oxides to synthesize excellent photocatalytic material.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO-ZnFe2O4
Metal-organic frameworks
Graphene quantum dots
Photocatalytic activity

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of pure ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4. (b) XPS survey spectra of ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4. High resolution XPS spectra of (c) Fe 2p, (d) Zn 2p, (e) O 1s, and (f) C 1s in corresponding samples.
SEM and TEM images of 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4: (a) SEM image; (b, c) TEM image with different magnification; (d) high resolution TEM image.
Photocatalytic degradation curves of MB over pure ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages under the irradiation of visible light (λ > 420 nm).
Photocatalytic degradation of MB over 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages with the addition of IPA, EDTA and Ar.
Photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline hydrochloride over pure ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages under the irradiation of visible light (λ > 420 nm).
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (a) and calculated band gap (b) of as-prepared samples obtained at ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4.
Photoluminescence (PL) spectra (a) and photocurrent generation (b) of ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4.
Schematic illustration of photocatalytic mechanism of 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages and their visible light photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Materials Utilization of Nonmetallic Minerals and Solid Wastes, National Laboratory of Mineral Materials, School of Materials Science and Technology, China University of Geosciences, 100083 Beijing, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Materials Utilization of Nonmetallic Minerals and Solid Wastes
School of Materials Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel graphene quantum dots (GQDs) decorated ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages ternary photocatalyst was prepared by a simple one-step deposition process. The as-prepared ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 were characterized by X-ray powder diffraction, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis, nitrogen absorption and desorption isothermal curves and electrochemical test. The synthesized GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance by degrading organic dye of methylene blue (MB) and tetracycline hydrochloride compared with ZnO and ZnO-ZnFe2O4. This study successfully shed a new thought on solving environmental problems by using low-cost, non-toxic metal oxides to synthesize excellent photocatalytic material.

A novel graphene quantum dots decorated ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages ternary photocatalyst has been prepared by a simple one-step deposition process for the first time.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, more and more people recognize the importance of water pollution treatment due to the environmental hazards caused by industry and agricultural pollutant [1–5]. In order to solve these problems, semiconductor photocatalysts are considered as one of important ways towards degradation of pollutants [6–8]. Among various semiconductors, metal oxides such as TiO2, ZnO have received great attention due to their excellent durability, non-toxicity, earth abundance and outstanding photocatalytic activities [9,10]. However, due to the large bandgap, those materials can only participate in photocatalytic activity by UV light which accounts for 4% of the solar spectrum [11]. Therefore, the effective use of visible light is still a challenge in photocatalytic applications. ZnFe2O4 as a well-behaved semiconductor material with a narrow bandgap (1.9 eV) and a strong visible light response, has widely aroused the attention of researchers [12]. However, ZnFe2O4 always shows poor photocatalytic activity because of its photo-generated electron-hole pairs are easy to combine [13,14]. At present, the research on ZnFe2O4 mainly focuses on the formation of heterojunctions [15] and ZnFe2O4-doped composite catalysts [16] to improve their photocatalytic activity.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have great potential in material design due to their tunable pores, high specific surface area and multifunctional functions. They can be assembled through coordination bonds between metal clusters and organic ligands [17]. In recent years, MOFs as a new precursor can synthesize porous oxide materials through a simple annealing process [18–21]. In this way, the microstructure and pore size of the material can be easily adjusted by controlling the annealing temperature and heating rate [22,23]. Therefore, we look forward to choose a suitable precursor to prepare ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocage by using suitable organic ligand.

Besides, the emerging graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have received great attention due to their unique and tunable photoluminescence properties, exceptional physicochemical properties, high photostability and small size [24]. GQDs with lateral sizes <100 nm have been considered to be better electron transporters and acceptors that make them as optimal candidates for photocatalytic materials [25]. Moreover, it has been an effective way for decorating graphene quantum dots to control recombination of photo-generated electron hole pairs. For example, Yan et al. reported BiOI/MnNb2O6 photocatalysts doped graphene quantum dots enhanced visible light efficiency [26]. Therefore, we hoped to prepare a new type of GQD-decorated ZnO-ZnFe2O4 ternary photocatalysts to further improve the photocatalytic properties of material.

In this study, we report a simple method to prepare ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages derived from MOF templates, which exhibited enhanced photocatalytic performance under visible light. To improve conductivity, we prepared the GQDs-loaded ZnO-ZnFe2O4 composite material by using charge-induced assembly method. The as-synthesized GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages exhibit excellent photocatalytic performance by degrading organic dye of methylene blue (MB) and tetracycline hydrochloride. The relationships between the photocatalyst structure and the photocatalytic activities are discussed in detail. The mechanism of pollutant photodegradation in this work is studied by using active species trapping experiments.

All reactants used in this study were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich without additional purification. In a typical synthesis procedure, 1.08 g acetylacetone iron (Fe(acac)3), 0.835 g zinc nitrate hydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), 0.17 g of terephthalic acid and 3.6 g of polyvinylpyrrolidone were added into a 500 mL beaker. Then, 240 mL of mixed solvent (dimethylformamide:ethanol = 5:3) were poured into this beaker, followed by stirring for 10 min to make them fully dissolved. Next, the mixture was transferred into a 500 mL 3-necked flask and refluxed at 100 °C for 6 h. A dark orange precipitate was collected, washed with dimethylformamide and ethanol for several times, and then dried at 80 °C for 24 h. In order to gain ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages, the above products were heated to 500 °C for 2 h in N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 1 °C min−1 and then cooled down to room temperature.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were purchased from XFNano company. The GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 was synthesized by one-step deposition process in room temperature. 20 mg ZnO/ZnFe2O4 sample was taken into the beaker mixed with 20 mL deionized water. Then the mixture was stirred vigorously for 10 min to disperse evenly. After that, different amount of GQDs were added dropwisely into the beaker under stirring condition. The GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 was collected, washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times for the following characterization. The 0.5GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4, 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 2GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 were named by adding the 0.5 mL, 1 mL and 2 mL of GQDs respectively.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy was performed on a Bruker D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (l = 1.5406 A°). The scanning step width of 0.02° and the scanning rate of 0.2 s−1 were applied to record the patterns in the 2θ range of 10–80°. The morphology and microstructure were obtained by an S-4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM) and a transmission electron microscopy (TEM JEM-2100F). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using the XPS spectrometers (ESCALab220I-XL). N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were obtained on an Autosorb-iQ-2MP automated gas sorption analyzer. Room temperature excitation and emission spectra were measured on a JOBIN 10 YVON FluoroMax-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) was obtained by thermal gravimetric analyzer (Q5000, TA). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were performed with sample powder from Perkin Elmer Lambda 35 UV–vis spectrometer.

Photocatalytic activities of ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 were evaluated by the degradation of methylene blue (MB, 20 mg/L). A 500 W Xenon lamp coupled with 420 nm cutoff filter was used to provide visible light irradiation. Powder photocatalyst (20 mg) was dispersed into 50 mL of MB solution. Before illumination, the solution was stirred in the dark for 1 h to achieve the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. After that, the light was turned on, and 3 mL of the suspension was taken at certain intervals and separated through centrifugation. The concentration of MB was analyzed by a Cary 5000 UV–vis spectrophotometer, thorough recording the absorbance of the characteristic peak at 664 nm.

For detecting the active species in the photocatalytic system, 1 mM ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), Ar atmosphere and 1 mM isopropanol (IPA) were added as the active species quenchers of the holes (h+), ·O2− and ·OH−, respectively. The method was similar to the former photocatalytic activity experiment.

The photocurrent measurement was performed on an electrochemical workstation (CHI-660E, China) consisting of a three-electrode quartz cell. 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution was used as the electrolyte. Platinum wires and saturated calomel electrodes (SCE) were used as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 film electrodes on ITO glass served as the working electrode. The electrochemical tests were measured at 0 V with a 300 W Xe lamp (a 420 nm cutoff filter was used) as the light source.

Fig. 1 shows that TGA curve of ZnO-ZnFe2O4 before annealing in order to understand the thermochemical process of the phase change reactions. Obviously, the loss (about 20%) of weight was believed to be caused by chemically adsorbed water. It was seen that nearly 55% of the mass loss was caused by the oxidation of organics. ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages were left because organics are completely oxidized at 500 °C. Therefore, the sintering temperature was chosen at 500 °C.

The structures of the as-synthesized ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 were characterized by XRD, as shown in Fig. 2a. The diffraction peaks at 31.77°, 34.42°, 36.25°, 47.54°, 56.60°,62.86° and 69.10° could be indexed to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103) and (112) planes of ZnO (JCPDS: 36–1451). The diffraction peaks at 18.19°, 29.92°, 35.26°, 42.84°, 53.11°,56.62° and 62.21° could be indexed to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (422),(511) and (440) planes of ZnFe2O4 (JCPDS: 22–1012). No characteristic peak of GQDs was detected from the 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages due to the relatively low content.

XPS was conducted for the 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages to reveal the chemical states of synthesized materials. The survey spectra in Fig. 2b confirmed that the ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages contain Zn, Fe, O, and C elements. The Fe 2p core level peaks at binding energies of 710.8 and 724.9 eV (Fig. 2c) indicated the existence of Fe3+. In Fig. 2d for Zn 2p spectra, the fitting peaks at 1044.9 and 1022.4 eV were related to Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2, respectively, indicating that the existence state of Zn element in ZnFe2O4 was Zn2+. The Fe 2p peak and Zn 2p peak in the 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages displayed a slight negative shift when compared to the corresponding peak in the ZnFe2O4. This indicated that there were chemical interactions between GQDs and ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages, which lead to the change of chemical environment of the surface Fe and Zn in 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4. The O 1s core level spectra showed a broad asymmetric curve fitted by two peaks with binding energies at 531.7 and 529.8 eV (see Fig. 2e), indicating the existence of two oxygen species. The main peak at 529.8 eV corresponds to oxygen ions in the crystal lattice, while the line located at 531.7 eV can be ascribed to the absorbed oxygen species. Fig. 2f showed the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s. The typical peak at 284.7 eV was ascribed to the CC bond with sp2 orbital. Peaks of the C 1s at the binding energies of 285.8 eV and 288.6 eV were attributed to the COC and CO, respectively. Therefore, the XPS results can confirm the coexistence of GQDs and ZnO-ZnFe2O4 in the 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages [27]. Fig. s1 shows the Raman spectra of 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4. A typical Raman spectra is resolved into two carbon related bands located at 1330 cm−1 (D band) and 1600 cm−1 (G band), respectively [28]. The Raman results can also confirm the coexistence of GQDs in the 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages. The result of FT-IR analysis (Fig. s2) was consistent with the XPS and Raman analysis [29–31].

The morphologies and microstructures of the 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 were represented by FESEM and TEM. As the SEM (Fig. 3a) shown, the 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 prepared by using metal-organic frameworks template method exhibited a uniformity hollow cubic structure with the width about 100 nm. Furthermore, it was still intact to keep this structure after annealing. To determine the optimal ratio of ethanol and dimethylformamide (DMF), we have prepared ZnO-ZnFe2O4 samples with different ratio of DMF to ethanol, including 0:8, 3:5 and 8:0. As shown in Fig. s3, when pure DMF or ethanol was used, only irregular nanoparticles were obtained (Fig. s3a and s3b). After the mixture solution of ethanol and DMF was used, the morphology changed to polyhedral structure [32]. This indicates that the mixed use of ethanol and DMF is important for the formation of ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages. From the TEM images (Fig. 3b, c), the shells of the nanocages were made up of numerous nanoparticles, with the diameter of about 15 nm. The porous frameworks existed on the surface would play the role of transport channels that allowed the reactants and products to pass through the shell. It was noted in Fig. 3d that the lattice spacing of 0.268 nm corresponded to the (002) crystal plane of ZnO and 0.476 nm corresponded well to the (111) planes of the cubic nanostructured ZnFe2O4. The interplanar spacing of GQDs was 0.295 nm, which was consistence with that of the graphite carbon source. EDS mapping technique was used to verify the distribution of various elements in the 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4. The elemental maps corresponding to Zn, Fe, C and O element were gathered in Fig. s4 and the results revealed that the Zn, Fe, C and O element are existed in the 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages.

Fig. 4 presented the photocatalytic degradation of MB over ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4, 0.5GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4, 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 2GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 under visible light irradiation. It could be obviously seen that all the GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 samples exhibited higher photocatalytic activity than the ZnO and ZnO-ZnFe2O4. The photodegradation efficiency of 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 reached 94% after 240 min of reaction. The degradation kinetics of MB by using as prepared samples was exhibited in Fig. 4b. The experimental data obviously shows that the apparent rate constant (k) was 0.00103, 0.00259, 0.00519, 0.00754 and 0.01021 min−1 for ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4, 2GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4, 0.5GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4, respectively. It is easy to know the photocatalytic activity of 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 was about 10 times and 4 times higher than that of ZnO and ZnO-ZnFe2O4. Furthermore, when the graphene quantum dots adding amount was increased (such as 2GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4), it exhibited low photodegradation performance due that excessive graphene quantum dots could reduce the absorption of sunlight. Fig. s5 shows the temporal evolution of the absorption spectra during the MB photodegradation over 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4. It can help us further understand the degradation process of MB.

From Fig. 5 , it was found that the photodegradation of MB was almost not affected through adding 1 mM IPA as a quencher of •OH. In contrast, the photocatalytic activity of 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages were largely suppressed by the addition of EDTA-2Na and Ar, and the inhibition efficiencies for degradation of MB are about 84% and 64%, respectively. Thus, it could be inferred that superoxide radicals (•O2−) and holes (h+) serves as the main active species for the photodegradation of MB over 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages under visible light irradiation.

Fig. 6a shows the degradation curves of tetracycline hydrochloride over pure ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4. The kinetics of tetracycline degradation of samples was studied and the results were exhibited in Fig. 6b. It can be shown that the apparent rate constant (k) was 0.02308, 0.04604 and 0.08809 min−1 for ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4, respectively. The photocatalytic activity of 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 was obviously higher than pure ZnO and ZnO-ZnFe2O4, which was consistent with MB decomposition results. Thus, the enhanced photocatalytic efficiency was ascribed to as-prepared hybrid photocatalyst instead of the MB photosentization mechanism.

The samples of ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 have similar specific surface area (nearly 60m2/g) due to the same preparation method and MOF templates as shown in Fig. s6. The UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 are shown in Fig. 7 . We can see the obvious absorption edge of the pure ZnO was only about 400 nm. Nevertheless, the ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 exhibited comparative photo-absorption with ZnO with absorption edges around 800 nm. That was to say that their absorption of light was obviously increased compared with ZnO. In fact, we can find that 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 had a narrower band gap semiconductor with the band gap was about 1.86 eV compared to the ZnO (2.98 eV) and ZnO-ZnFe2O4 (1.88 eV) from Fig. 7b. The bandgap value of semiconductor materials can be affected by factors such as size, doping material and oxygen vacancies [33]. Here, the band gap of ZnO measured by DRS was 2.98 eV, which is lower than the reported band gap of 3.3 eV. This would be due to the contribution of ZnO large size nanoparticles [34]. The narrowest band gap energy for 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 would result that it could absorb more photons and generate more photo-produced electrons than pure ZnO and ZnO-ZnFe2O4 under identical light irradiation.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were a useful technique to survey the separation efficiency of the photogenerated charge carriers in a semiconductor. Fig. 8a shows the PL spectra of the ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4. The emission peaks of ZnO located at 410 and 470 nm are derived from interface radiation defects [35]. After the formation of ZnO-ZnFe2O4 composite, the PL intensity decreases obviously. As we know, the lower the PL intensity, the lower the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The results indicate that the ZnO-ZnFe2O4 heterojunction can effectively promote the separation of photogenerated carriers [36]. Furthermore, the 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 shows the lowest PL signal among the three samples. This illustrates that graphene quantum dots as an electron acceptor can further increase the charge transfer and reduce the recombination process [37]. Fig. 8b shows the photocurrent response of ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 with or without visible light illumination. Obviously, the current abruptly increased and decreased as switching the light source on and off. The photocurrent response of the pure ZnO and ZnO-ZnFe2O4 were 0.4 and 0.5 μA, far behind that of 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages (2.8 μA). In other words, the photocurrent response of 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages was about 7 times higher than others. The results showed that the photogenerated electron-hole pairs of 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 could be separated more effectively than that of pure ZnO and ZnO-ZnFe2O4, confirming the positive effect of GQDs on photogenerated charge carrier separation. This was consistent with the results from the above photodegradation experiments.

Based on the above analysis, we can discuss about the mechanism of enhancing photocatalytic performance of 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4. Scheme 1 shows the schematic representation of photocatalytic mechanism of 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 during the photocatalytic process. Under the irradiation of sunlight, the photo-generated electrons and holes were formed because of electrons transition. The photo-generated electrons could combine with oxygen that formed ·OH and ·O2 −. Lastly, organic matter was broken down into H2O and CO2 by the ·OH, ·O2 − and holes. There were two main reasons for improving the photocatalytic properties of 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4. Firstly, the band gap of 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 was significantly reduced due to the heterostructure formed and graphene quantum dots doped that increasing the absorption of visible light. Secondly, the existence of graphene quantum dots could increase the charge transfer rate of the material and provide more reactive sites, thereby improving the photocatalytic performance.

In conclusion, we report a simple method to prepare GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages derived from metal-organic framework nanocages templates. The photodegradation performance of 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 nanocages was significantly better than those of the ZnO, ZnO-ZnFe2O4, 0.5GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 and 2GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4. We ascribed the excellent performance of 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 to the following reasons: (1) The band gap of 1GQD/ZnO-ZnFe2O4 were significantly reduced due to the formed heterostructure; (2) The graphene quantum dots were considered as an electron reservoir and transporter and as a powerful energy transfer component in the photocatalytic process.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2652015086).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.01.151.
